ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for chendol or Thai chendol; mapped to the nearest parent class 'dessert food product' as a defensible approximate anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for chendol; A0CKP (coconut-based dessert) is the closest defensible category anchor. Exact match unavailable.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for chendol or Thai-style pandan jelly dessert; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai Chendol weighs approximately 300 g and contains pandan jelly noodles, coconut milk, palm sugar syrup, and shaved ice.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore HPB Foodo — Cendol (coconut milk dessert) and component-level estimation for Thai-style variant
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai Chendol exists in HPB Foodo. Values are adapted from HPB Foodo cendol / coconut milk dessert entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central coconut milk (SR Legacy #12117) and tapioca starch (SR Legacy #20068) component data. The input record's per_100g values (energy 126.67 kcal, protein 1.17 g, fat 7.33 g, carbohydrate 15.0 g, sugar 12.67 g, sodium 16.67 mg, dietary fibre 0.67 g, saturated fat 6.0 g) are consistent with HPB cendol reference ranges and are retained with medium confidence. Calcium 6.67 mg and iron 0.17 mg are within plausible ranges for coconut milk-based desserts per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; tapioca/rice starch-based dessert analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai Chendol or standard cendol in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 78 based on the closest analogous foods: tapioca pudding (GI ~81, University of Sydney GI Database) and rice flour-based desserts (GI ~72–85). Palm sugar has a GI of approximately 54 (University of Sydney GI Database), but the dominant starch component (pandan jelly noodles from rice/tapioca flour) drives the overall GI to the high range. Weighted estimate of 78 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (78 × 45.0) / 100 = 35.1 (carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 15.0 × 3 = 45.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar content (12.67 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (0.67 g/100 g); palm sugar-driven insulin-mediated sodium retention
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should avoid Thai Chendol; the 12.67 g/100 g sugar load (38 g per 300 g serving) independently elevates blood pressure risk via insulin-mediated sodium retention per AHA 2018 guidelines, and the high saturated fat from coconut milk (6.0 g/100 g) compounds cardiovascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~78), high sugar (12.67 g/100 g), high glycaemic load per serving (35.1), minimal dietary fibre (0.67 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Thai Chendol is unsuitable for patients with Type 2 Diabetes; the estimated GI of 78 combined with a glycaemic load of 35.1 per 300 g serving causes rapid and significant postprandial glucose elevation, with pandan jelly noodles (high-GI refined starch) and palm sugar syrup providing minimal fibre buffering.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g from coconut milk (18 g per 300 g serving) and high added sugar (12.67 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Thai Chendol poses high cardiovascular risk; the saturated fat content of 6.0 g/100 g (18 g per serving) from coconut milk significantly exceeds the AHA recommended daily limit, and the high added sugar load (38 g per serving) further elevates LDL cholesterol and triglyceride levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100 g from coconut milk exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold; high added sugar promotes pro-inflammatory mediators
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Thai Chendol; saturated fat at 6.0 g/100 g from coconut milk triggers TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokines, and the 12.67 g/100 g added sugar from palm sugar syrup further promotes systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Chendol (泰式煎蕊; Thai: เฉาก๊วยไทย) is a chilled Southeast Asian dessert of Thai origin, popular in Singapore and across the region. It consists of vibrant green pandan-flavoured jelly noodles made from rice flour or tapioca starch, served in a bowl with rich coconut milk, dark palm sugar syrup (gula melaka), and shaved ice. The dessert is assembled cold without cooking at the point of service. The texture is a contrast of soft, slippery jelly strands, creamy coconut milk, and crunchy ice, with a flavour profile that is sweet, fragrant with pandan, and mildly rich from the coconut. Nutritionally, Thai Chendol is energy-dense at approximately 127 kcal per 100 g, with a high sugar content of 12.67 g/100 g and saturated fat of 6.0 g/100 g from coconut milk. Dietary fibre is low at 0.67 g/100 g. The estimated glycaemic index is high (~78) and the glycaemic load per standard 300 g serving is approximately 35.1, making it clinically unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. It is naturally gluten-free, dairy-free, vegan, vegetarian, and halal. The dish is commonly consumed as a dessert or afternoon snack and is widely available at hawker centres, food courts, and dessert stalls across Singapore and Thailand.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Thai Chendol?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai Chendol exists in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB cendol/coconut milk dessert reference data and USDA SR Legacy component data for coconut milk and tapioca starch. The input values are consistent with these references and are retained with medium confidence. GI is an analogous estimate based on tapioca pudding and rice flour dessert GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation, micronutrient, and moisture data for Thai-style chendol are not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — coconut milk and plant-based ingredients contain no dietary cholesterol per USDA SR Legacy). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no vitamin C-contributing ingredients are present in standard Thai Chendol formulation per USDA SR Legacy). trans_fat_g is 0.03 (trace level retained from input; consistent with negligible trans fat in coconut milk per USDA SR Legacy). Nutrient values adapted from HPB Foodo cendol reference and USDA SR Legacy coconut milk (#12117) and tapioca starch (#20068) component data. GI of 78 is an analogous estimate based on tapioca pudding (GI ~81) and rice flour-based dessert (GI ~72–85) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chendol is available. Food group reclassified from 'Beverages' to 'Desserts' as chendol is a solid-component chilled dessert consumed with a spoon, not a drinkable beverage.
